title: Docker & Container Lifecycle
description: Docker is the most widely used container runtime, built on Linux cgroups and namespaces. At FAANG, Docker (or its OCI-compatible alternatives like containerd) is the standard unit of deployment. Understanding the container lifecycle — from building to running to debugging — is essential for any infrastructure engineer.

sections.title: Container Images: Layers & Build Caching
sections.description: A Docker image is a stack of read-only layers, each representing a change to the filesystem. Layers are cached and shared between images — this is why pulling updates is fast (only new layers are downloaded). At FAANG, image layer optimization is critical for deployment speed: minimizing layers, caching dependencies, and using distroless base images reduce both build time and attack surface.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Dockerfile best practices ===

# 1. Use specific base image versions (not 'latest')
FROM ubuntu:22.04@sha256:abc123...

# 2. Combine RUN commands to minimize layers
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \\
    python3 python3-pip \\
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# 3. Use .dockerignore (like .gitignore for builds)
# .dockerignore:
# node_modules
# .git
# *.log

# 4. Multi-stage builds (separate build deps from runtime)
FROM node:22 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production

FROM node:22-alpine AS runtime
COPY --from=builder /app /app
USER node
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

# 5. Use distroless or alpine for smaller images
# Distroless: gcr.io/distroless/nodejs22-debian12
# Alpine: node:22-alpine (much smaller, but uses musl libc)

# 6. Don't run as root inside the container
RUN addgroup -S myapp && adduser -S myapp -G myapp
USER myapp

# === Build optimization ===
# Cache layers that change infrequently (package.json → deps first, then source)
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci            # This layer only rebuilds when package.json changes
COPY . .              # This layer rebuilds every time (frequent changes)

# === Layer inspection ===
docker history nginx              # Show layer sizes and commands
docker inspect nginx              # Detailed image metadata (layers, env, ports)

# === Image size commands ===
docker images --format "table {{.Repository}}\t{{.Tag}}\t{{.Size}}"
docker system df                  # Total disk usage of Docker

sections.title: Container Runtime & Lifecycle
sections.description: When Docker runs a container, it goes through: pull image → create container → create namespaces → set up cgroups → mount rootfs → apply seccomp → exec CMD. Understanding this sequence helps you debug startup failures and performance issues. At FAANG, the container runtime is usually containerd (the industry-standard OCI runtime) with runc as the low-level runtime.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Container lifecycle commands ===
# Build:
docker build -t myapp:latest .       # Build image from Dockerfile
docker build --no-cache -t myapp .   # Force rebuild (ignore cache)

# Run:
docker run -d --name myapp -p 8080:80 myapp:latest
docker run -it --rm ubuntu:22.04 bash  # Interactive, auto-remove

# Stop/start:
docker stop myapp                    # SIGTERM + wait + SIGKILL
docker start myapp                   # Restart stopped container
docker restart myapp                 # Stop + start
docker kill myapp                    # SIGKILL immediately

# Inspect:
docker ps                            # Running containers
docker ps -a                         # All containers (including stopped)
docker logs -f myapp                 # Follow logs
docker logs --tail 100 myapp         # Last 100 lines
docker inspect myapp                 # Full container metadata (JSON)

# Execute in running container:
docker exec -it myapp bash           # Start shell in container
docker exec myapp ps aux             # Run command in container

# === Resource limits ===
docker run -d --name db \
  --memory=512M \
  --memory-reservation=256M \
  --cpus=0.5 \
  --pids-limit=200 \
  --ulimit nofile=65536:65536 \
  postgres:16

# === Health checks ===
# In Dockerfile:
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --retries=3 \
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:8080/health || exit 1

# Manual check:
docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}' myapp

# === Copy files ===
docker cp myapp:/var/log/app.log ./local.log
docker cp ./config.yaml myapp:/etc/app/config.yaml

# === Cleanup ===
docker rm myapp                      # Remove container
docker rmi myapp:latest              # Remove image
docker system prune -a               # Remove ALL unused containers, images, networks

sections.title: Networking & Storage in Docker
sections.description: Docker provides several network drivers (bridge for single-host, overlay for multi-host, host for performance, macvlan for direct MAC assignment) and storage options (volumes for persistent data, bind mounts for host directories, tmpfs for temporary memory-backed storage). At FAANG, overlay networks (Cilium, Flannel, Calico) handle container networking, and cloud block storage (EBS volumes) is mounted via bind mounts.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Network drivers ===
# Bridge (default): containers connect via docker0 bridge
docker network create mynet          # Create custom bridge network
docker run --network=mynet nginx

# Host: container uses host's network stack (no isolation, best performance)
docker run --network=host nginx

# Overlay: multi-host networking (Docker Swarm or Kubernetes)
docker network create -d overlay myoverlay

# === Port mapping ===
docker run -p 8080:80 nginx          # host:8080 → container:80

# === DNS within Docker ===
# Containers on same user-defined bridge can resolve each other by name
# docker run --name web --network=mynet nginx
# docker run --network=mynet alpine ping web  # Works!

# === Volumes ===
docker volume create mydata           # Create volume
docker run -v mydata:/var/lib/data postgres  # Mount volume

# Bind mount:
docker run -v /host/path:/container/path nginx

# tmpfs (RAM storage):
docker run --tmpfs /tmp:size=100M nginx

# === Backup volume ===
docker run --rm -v mydata:/source -v $(pwd):/backup alpine tar czf /backup/mydata.tar.gz -C /source .

# === Storage drivers ===
# overlay2 (default, modern) — Copy-on-Write, fast
# aufs (legacy) — Older Ubuntu default
# devicemapper — RHEL legacy (slow, avoid)
docker info | grep Storage

# === Container resource inspection ===
docker stats                        # Live resource usage
# CPU%, MEM%, MEM USAGE/LIMIT, NET I/O, BLOCK I/O, PIDS

docker stats --no-stream --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.CPUPerc}}\t{{.MemUsage}}"

sections.title: Debugging Containers
sections.description: Containers introduce debugging challenges: you're running in an isolated environment with limited tools. At FAANG, debugging containers requires understanding both the application inside and the host outside. Key tools: docker exec for inside, nsenter for entering namespaces from host, and cgroup inspections for resource issues.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Inside-container debugging ===
docker exec -it myapp bash            # Shell inside container

# Install debugging tools (temporary container):
docker run --rm -it --net=container:myapp --pid=container:myapp \
  nicolaka/netshoot:latest            # Network troubleshooting toolbox

# === Host-level debugging ===
# Find container PID:
CONTAINER_PID=$(docker inspect --format '{{.State.Pid}}' myapp)
echo $CONTAINER_PID

# Enter container namespaces from host:
sudo nsenter -t $CONTAINER_PID -n ip addr  # Network namespace
sudo nsenter -t $CONTAINER_PID -p ps aux   # PID namespace
sudo nsenter -t $CONTAINER_PID -a bash     # All namespaces

# Check container cgroup:
CGROUP_PATH=$(cat /proc/$CONTAINER_PID/cgroup | grep -oP '/system.slice/docker-.*\.scope')
cat /sys/fs/cgroup$CGROUP_PATH/memory.events

# === Container logs ===
docker logs myapp                     # All logs
docker logs --tail 50 --follow --timestamps myapp

# === Check container filesystem ===
docker diff myapp                     # Show changed files (A=added, C=changed, D=deleted)

# === Export container filesystem ===
docker export myapp > container.tar   # Export as tar archive
docker import container.tar           # Import as image

# === Debug entrypoint/cmd ===
# Override entrypoint to debug:
docker run -it --entrypoint sh myapp:latest

# === Common issues ===
# 1. Container exits immediately: check CMD/ENTRYPOINT and logs
# 2. Port already in use: change host port mapping
# 3. Out of disk space: docker system prune -a
# 4. Permission denied inside: check USER directive and volume permissions
# 5. DNS resolution fails: check --dns or /etc/docker/daemon.json

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'Your containerized application is running slow in production. How do you debug?'

A: 'First, rule out resource limits. Check docker stats or the cgroup metrics: cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu.stat (throttling), memory.events (OOM), io.stat (I/O throttling). If the container is hitting limits, increase them or optimize the app. Next, check the host: top for CPU, iostat for disk, free -h for memory. If the host is fine, debug inside the container: docker exec -it myapp bash, then use standard Linux tools (top, strace, lsof). If the container lacks tools, use nsenter from the host. At FAANG, we use ephemeral debug containers — a sidecar with network and PID namespace sharing that contains all debugging tools, without modifying the production container.'

Q: 'How would you design a container image for production?'

A: 'Production container image requirements: (1) Use a specific base image version, pinned by digest, not tag. (2) Multi-stage build: builder stage with build tools, runtime stage with only the binary and minimal dependencies. (3) Non-root user — never run as root. (4) HEALTHCHECK instruction. (5) No secrets in the image — use Docker build secrets or runtime secrets injection. (6) Minimal size: use distroless or Alpine base, combined RUN layers, clean package manager cache. (7) Reproducible: exact same image for the same git commit. (8) Signed: use Docker Content Trust or cosign for image signing. At FAANG, image compliance is enforced by admission controllers in the container registry — images without a HEALTHCHECK, running as root, or with known vulnerabilities are rejected.'

Q: 'What's the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT in a Dockerfile?'

A: 'ENTRYPOINT defines the executable that always runs. CMD provides default arguments to ENTRYPOINT. ENTRYPOINT ["nginx"] + CMD ["-g", "daemon off;"] = runs nginx -g 'daemon off;'. If you override CMD at runtime (docker run nginx -t), it becomes nginx -t (CMD replaced). If you override ENTRYPOINT (docker run --entrypoint bash nginx), it runs bash instead of nginx. Best practice: use ENTRYPOINT for the fixed binary and CMD for the default arguments — this makes the container behavior predictable while allowing argument customization.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Dockerfile Best Practices — Official Docker documentation
  • Docker Compose — Multi-container orchestration (development & testing)
  • containerd documentation — Industry-standard container runtime used in Kubernetes

details:
FAANG Architect's Note: Docker is the 'hello world' of containers — most FAANG environments use containerd or CRI-compatible runtimes directly, not the Docker CLI. But the concepts are identical: Docker taught the industry how to think about containers. The Dockerfile format, image layering, and the run/build model are universal. Learn Docker first; the transition to containerd/nerdctl/buildkit is straightforward because the underlying principles are the same.

Production Tip: Never run 'docker build' on a production server. Build images in your CI/CD pipeline, push them to a registry, and pull them on production nodes. This ensures that: (1) builds are reproducible (same commit = same image), (2) build tools aren't on production servers, (3) images are scanned for vulnerabilities before deployment, (4) rollbacks are instant (just point to the previous image tag). At FAANG, image building and deployment are entirely separate stages — building happens in a hermetic build environment, deployment pulls from a registry.
